critique.summary: The article presents Zeroboot, a project that implements sub-millisecond virtual machine (VM) sandboxes using copy-on-write (CoW) memory forking. It highlights the performance benchmarks, architecture, and SDKs available for running isolated VM sandboxes efficiently, targeting AI agents and code execution use cases.
mainPoints: Zeroboot creates VM sandboxes with sub-millisecond spawn latency using CoW memory forking.
Each sandbox is a real KVM virtual machine with hardware-enforced memory isolation.
Performance benchmarks show Zeroboot achieves significantly lower spawn latency and memory usage compared to other microsandboxes.
The system uses Firecracker snapshots as templates for fast VM forking.
SDKs are available in Python and TypeScript for easy integration.
The project is a working prototype but not yet production-hardened.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Zeroboot's architecture and performance benchmarks align with known techniques in virtualization and sandboxing, such as using KVM and Firecracker snapshots with copy-on-write memory to achieve fast VM instantiation. The provided benchmark numbers are plausible given the technology described. However, the project is presented as a prototype, and independent verification of benchmarks and security isolation would be necessary for production use. The article does not cite external sources or third-party evaluations, so while the technical claims are consistent with existing virtualization concepts, the performance and security assertions remain unverified beyond the author's data.
considerations: The security guarantees of the sandbox depend on the underlying KVM and Firecracker implementations and require thorough auditing.
Performance benchmarks are provided by the project author and lack independent validation.
The project is not production-hardened, so stability and robustness in real-world scenarios are uncertain.
Comparison with other sandboxing technologies should consider trade-offs in complexity, compatibility, and security.
The impact of workload type on sandbox performance is not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the technical merits and challenges of sub-millisecond VM sandboxes using copy-on-write memory forking. Commenters highlight security concerns like entropy duplication, practical deployment issues such as networking setup and snapshot invalidation, and potential use cases and comparisons to existing technologies.

comments.author: cperciva
comments.text:
Don't forget about entropy! You've just created two identical copies of all of your random number generators, which could be very very bad for security.

The firecracker team wrote a very good paper about addressing this when they added snapshot support.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies a critical security risk related to entropy duplication in VM cloning, a well-known issue addressed in related research.

comments.author: wei03288
comments.text:
The CoW forking approach makes sense for the warm path, but I'm curious how snapshot invalidation works when the base VM state changes — say, a dependency update or config change. With traditional VM snapshots you typically rebuild the snapshot manually; CoW forks from a live parent mean you have to decide when the parent itself gets updated and whether in-flight child VMs get migrated or just drain.

The 258KB per-VM memory figure is impressive, but the real question for production use is what happens to the parent process under high fork rates — does the CoW page table overhead accumulate linearly or is there a cleanup mechanism that runs async?

Strong result either way. The startup time numbers make this actually viable for per-request sandbox isolation, which most approaches can't pull off at that latency.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully discusses practical challenges of snapshot invalidation and resource overhead, showing a deep understanding of production implications.

comments.author: crawshaw
comments.text:
Nice to see this work! I experimented with this for exe.dev before we launched. The VM itself worked really well, but there was a lot of setup to get the networking functioning. And in the end, our target are use cases that don't mind a ~1-second startup time, which meant doing a clean systemd start each time was easier.

That said, I have seen several use cases where people want a VM for something minimal, like a python interpreter, and this is absolutely the sort of approach they should be using. Lot of promise here, excited to see how far you can push it!
comments.analysis: Provides valuable real-world experience with similar technology, highlighting networking complexity and relevant use cases.

comments.author: diptanu
comments.text: The tricky part of doing this in production is cloning sandboxes across nodes. You would have to snapshot the resident memory, file system (or a CoW layer on top of the rootfs), move the data across nodes, etc.
comments.analysis: Accurately points out operational challenges in distributed environments, emphasizing the complexity of state synchronization.

comments.author: vmg12
comments.text:
Does it only work with that specific version of firecracker and only with vms with 1 vcpu?

More than the sub ms startup time the 258kb of ram per VM is huge.
comments.analysis: Raises valid questions about compatibility and resource usage, though the claim about 258KB RAM being huge depends on context.

comments.author: buckle8017
comments.text: This is how android processes work, but it's a security problem breaking some ASLR type things.
comments.analysis: Makes a reasonable analogy to Android process management and notes security implications, though details are sparse.

comments.author: indigodaddy
comments.text: Does this need passthrough or might we be able to leverage PVM with it on a passthrough-less cloud VM/VPS?
comments.analysis: Asks a relevant technical question about hardware virtualization features but does not provide an answer.

comments.author: indigodaddy
comments.text:
Your write-up made me think of:

https://codesandbox.io/blog/how-we-clone-a-running-vm-in-2-s...

Are there parallels?
comments.analysis: Draws a useful comparison to another VM cloning approach, encouraging further exploration.

comments.author: latortuga
comments.analysis: Briefly suggests a comparison to another project, but lacks elaboration.

comments.author: handfuloflight
comments.text: Can you run this in another sandbox? Not sure why you'd want to... but can you?
comments.analysis: Poses a speculative question about sandbox nesting without providing insight or evidence.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.analysis: A meta-comment about thread management that does not contribute to technical discussion.
